questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic resource provisioning in virtual clusters. When would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Static provisioning allocates fixed resources upfront, dynamic adjusts based on real-time demand
Static is simpler to implement and predict, but may waste resources during low demand
Dynamic provisioning enables elasticity and cost optimization but requires monitoring and automation
Choose static for predictable workloads (databases, legacy apps)
Choose dynamic for variable workloads (web apps, batch processing)

questions.question: Describe the First-Fit Decreasing (FFD) algorithm for VM placement. Why is it preferred over First-Fit in virtual cluster consolidation?
keyPoints: FFD sorts VMs in descending order of resource requirements before placement
First-Fit places each VM in the first host with sufficient capacity
FFD achieves better bin utilization (approximately 11/9 × optimal for 1D)
Reduces number of active hosts, lowering energy consumption
Minimizes fragmentation compared to unordered First-Fit

questions.question: What is the trade-off between CPU overcommitment and performance isolation in virtual clusters? How can administrators balance these?
keyPoints: Overcommitment increases utilization but causes contention when VMs compete for resources
Performance isolation techniques: CPU shares, limits, reservations, vCPU pinning
Balance by understanding workload characteristics (latency-sensitive vs batch)
Monitor performance metrics and adjust overcommitment ratios accordingly
Use reservation for critical VMs, limit for noisy neighbors

questions.question: Explain NUMA awareness in virtual cluster scheduling. Why is it important and how is it implemented?
keyPoints: NUMA architectures have non-uniform memory access latency based on CPU-memory distance
VMs spanning NUMA nodes suffer 3-10× memory access latency for remote pages
Implementation: pin VMs to single NUMA node, or use 'prefer nearest' scheduling
Benefits: predictable performance, reduced latency for memory-intensive workloads
Tools: numactl, ESXi NUMA scheduling, Kubernetes topology managers

questions.question: A company has 50 VMs with average CPU utilization of 20% on a 20-host cluster. Propose a consolidation strategy to reduce costs while maintaining performance.
keyPoints: Calculate current utilization: 50 VMs × 20% = 10 VM-equivalents
With typical 50% safety margin, can host on 10-12 VMs per server
Consolidation ratio: reduce from 50 to ~15-20 VMs per host
Use FFD or best-fit decreasing for placement
Implement live migration and monitoring to ensure SLA compliance
Consider right-sizing VMs with lower utilization

questions.question: Compare Kubernetes, Mesos, and vSphere as virtual cluster management platforms. Discuss their architecture, scheduling approaches, and suitable use cases.
keyPoints: Kubernetes: container orchestrator, declarative desired state, pod-based scheduling, ideal for cloud-native microservices
Mesos: distributed systems kernel, two-level scheduling (Mesos + frameworks), suitable for hybrid workloads with Marathon/Spark
vSphere: hypervisor-centric, DRS for VM placement, vMotion for migration, ideal for enterprise virtualization with existing VMware infrastructure
Scheduling: Kubernetes uses kube-scheduler with predicates/priorities, Mesos uses offer-based filtering, vSphere uses DRS with DPM
Use cases: Kubernetes for containerized cloud-native, Mesos for big data + containers, vSphere for traditional VM workloads requiring strong isolation

questions.question: Design a resource management framework for a multi-tenant virtual cluster supporting diverse workloads (web servers, batch analytics, databases). Include provisioning, scheduling, isolation, and SLA enforcement mechanisms.
keyPoints: Provisioning: Separate resource pools per tenant with guaranteed reservations, elastic expansion up to limits
Scheduling: Workload-aware - batch on low-priority nodes, databases with NUMA awareness, web servers with auto-scaling
Isolation: Network (VLANs, security groups), CPU (shares/limits), Memory (reservations), I/O (QoS queues)
SLA enforcement: Monitor SLA metrics, implement dynamic scaling, use predictive analytics for capacity planning
Energy management: Consolidate during off-peak, migrate to fewer hosts, power down unused servers
Tools integration: Kubernetes for containers, VMware/vSphere for VMs, unified monitoring with Prometheus/Grafana
Compliance: Audit logging, quota enforcement, billing based on usage
